title: Traveller's notebook insert construction
subtitle: Making a folded-signature insert for a Midori-style notebook cover
excerpt: A traveller's notebook insert is a small pamphlet-bound booklet that slips into the elastic of a leather or fabric cover. Making your own inserts lets you choose the paper weight, ruling, and page count. This tutorial covers a single-signature insert sewn through three holes, sized for the standard A5 and passport formats.
sourceNotes: Single-signature pamphlet binding is a long-established bookbinding technique documented in multiple public-domain bookbinding manuals. The traveller's notebook format is a twentieth-century development associated with Midori Co., Japan; the insert dimensions and binding method described here are independent of that trademark.

glossaryTerms.definition: A folded group of sheets sewn through the fold to form the pages of a pamphlet or booklet. A single-signature insert has all its pages in one nested fold.
A sewing method in which thread passes in and out through holes punched along the fold of a signature. The simplest bookbinding stitch, producing a strong spine visible from outside.
Card or heavy paper (160 gsm and above) used as the outer cover of a pamphlet. Protects the text pages and provides a surface for decoration or labelling.

content.text: A traveller's notebook insert is a self-contained

pamphlet that sits inside the elastics of a cover. Most commercial inserts are 48 to 64 pages of thin paper. Making your own allows a heavier cartridge paper for drawing, a dotted paper for journalling, or a mixed-media paper for watercolour. The
used here requires only a needle, thread, and three pierced holes.

attrs.title: Craft knife safety
attrs.body: Cut away from fingers, keep the blade low-angle to the mat, and replace blades when they drag rather than slice.

attrs.heading: For one standard A5 insert (105 mm × 148 mm folded)

items.name: Text-weight paper, 80 to 100 gsm, for interior pages
items.qty: 5 to 8 sheets A4, each folding to 4 pages

items.name: Cover-weight paper or thin card, 160 to 200 gsm
Waxed linen thread or bookbinding thread
Awl or thick needle for piercing

content.text: The standard A5 traveller's notebook insert finishes at approximately 105 mm wide by 148 mm tall when closed. To make this from A4 paper: cut each A4 sheet to 210 mm wide by 148 mm tall (half the A4 height). Fold each sheet in half widthwise using a bone folder to make crisp folds: 105 mm × 148 mm, four pages per sheet. Nest all the folded sheets inside the folded cover, keeping pages in the order you want.

Mark three sewing stations along the fold of the nested signature: one at the centre, one 15 mm from the top, one 15 mm from the bottom. Open the signature to its full height and pierce through all layers at each station using an awl or thick needle. Thread the needle with 60 cm of waxed thread. Enter from outside through the centre hole, then pass to the top hole (staying inside), exit through the top hole, re-enter through the centre, pass to the bottom hole, exit, and return to the centre hole. Tie off with a square knot between the two thread tails at the centre hole, then trim the tails to 10 mm. This is the

sheet to the same dimensions as the text pages before folding: 210 mm × 148 mm. Fold it around the outside of the signature and sew through at the same three stations as the text pages. The cover wraps the spine and extends 2 to 3 mm beyond the text block on the fore-edge when closed.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Problems in traveller's notebook insert construction.

items.symptom: The finished insert is too wide to fit inside the notebook cover elastics
items.cause: The folded pages are slightly wider than the target size, or the paper is too heavy and does not fold tightly.
items.fix: Trim 2 mm from the fore-edge of the finished insert with a craft knife and ruler. The trimmed edge will look cleaner than a torn one.

items.symptom: The thread pulls through the pierced holes when the knot is tightened
items.cause: The holes were pierced too large, or the thread weight is too light for the number of pages.
items.fix: Pierce with a finer awl for a tighter hole. Use waxed linen thread rather than standard thread; the wax grips the paper fibres and reduces pull-through.
